Tafseer:
The Quran is the greatest blessing of Allah:
Just as the verbs "Ta'adum" and "Ta'ali" and other terms of this pattern convey the meaning of exaggeration and intensity, similarly "Tabarak" (Blessed) contains the meaning of exaggeration. That is: highly blessed and generous is the Entity who, for the warning of mankind, sent down to His servant a Book which holds the status of a decisive criterion and proof to distinguish between truth and falsehood. People should appreciate this blessing and reform their errors in the light of this Book, rather than raising various kinds of objections to oppose it and demanding some miracle or sign of punishment from the Messenger of Allah. If Allah willed, instead of this Book, He could have sent His Messenger armed with some whip of punishment. This work was not difficult for Him at all, but He, out of His great mercy, approved showing people a light that guides them to the path of success and prosperity by warning them of the upcoming dangers. By expressing the Quran by the word "Furqan" (Criterion), pointer is made to the reality: this Book is in itself the proof and argument to prove its own claim and the truthfulness of its presenter—it is not in need of any external proof. Wretched are those who, instead of benefiting from this greatest blessing distributed through the hands of the Messenger, demand from him such things which become a cause of destruction for them instead of good.

A specific timely address for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him):
In "'ala 'abdihi" (upon His servant), there is a specific address for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). This address has a specific timely context here. Forward, those objections of the disbelievers are quoted which they made in a highly contemptuous manner against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). These objections were raised mostly by the wealthy of Makkah and Ta'if. They, in the pride of their worldly superiority, specifically targeted the Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) detachment from worldly means and resources, presenting this as a major proof to refute his mission. Keeping this mindset of the arrogant in view, Allah said: "After the greatest blessing Allah has sent down upon His specific servant in the form of the Furqan, he is not in need of anything. For the completion of the mission of warning mankind on which he is appointed, whatever provision and resources he is in need of are fully present with him."

This verse is the introduction of this Surah. Its implications which we have explained will become more clear, if God wills, from the upcoming discussions.

The Quran is not the request of any petitioner, but the obligatory decree and call of the Creator of the universe:
In the previous verse, Allah pointed to His generosity and favor. Now, He has referenced His Monotheism and majesty, whose purpose is to remind of the reality: He who has sent down this Book for the warning and reminder of mankind is He in whose grasp of power is the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth; for this reason, let no one consider this Book as the request of a petitioner; rather, this is the obligatory decree of the True Sovereign of this universe. If it is denied, He who sent it down is no weak entity to take revenge for its denial. He will take revenge, and when He takes, no one will be able to hold His hand.

Dispelling a misunderstanding:
"Walam yattakhidh waladan walam yakun lahu shareekun fee al-mulki"—meaning, if anyone has assumed sons and daughters for Him, starting their worship, and is under the delusion that they will save him from God's grasp, let him remove this false assumption from the heart. Neither does God have any daughter, nor any son, nor is any other partner in His sovereignty. He is the sole Owner of His sovereignty, and He is not in need of any helper or partner.

The proof of God's Monotheism:
"Wa khalaqa kulla shay'in faqaddarahu taqdeeran" (And He created everything and determined it with [precise] determination)—this is the proof of His Monotheism and uniqueness described: He created everything and has prescribed a measure for everything. It is not possible for anything to decrease, increase, advance, or delay from His prescribed measure. He created man, and prescribed a limit of life and death for him—no one can go out of this limit. The clouds and wind are all bound to His prescribed limits. The sun and moon, whom the fools worshipped as deities, are bound to a specific orbit and axis, testifying through their existence that they are created by a Wise and Powerful God and bound to His prescribed limits. To this reality, attention is directed in another place:
(And the sun runs [on course] toward its stopping point. That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing.) [Ya-Sin: 38]

In even more spacious words, this same is said thus:
(And there is not a thing but that with Us are its depositories, and We do not send it down except in a known measure.) [Al-Hijr: 21]

This is the further explanation of the same theme, and in it is also the implicit expression of surprise: the actual reality is indeed what has been explained, but the state of these opponents of the Quran and the Messenger is: they have taken deities besides God who not only are incapable of creating anything, but are themselves created by God, and who, let alone bringing any harm or benefit to others, do not possess the power to protect themselves from any harm or bring any benefit; and who possess no choice over death, nor life, nor raising alive after death!... The meaning is: to deny the realities presented by the Quran on the strength of such helpless assumed deities is merely their loss of intellect.

The propaganda of the Quraysh leaders to make the public bad-thinking of the Quran:
These are the statements of the opponents quoted which they spread to make people bad-thinking of the Quran and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). We have mentioned in another place: as far as the force of expression of the Quran and its power and influence are concerned, the leaders of the Quraysh did not have the courage to deny them. They were forced to admit it. However, their effort was: the assumption of the Quran being a heavenly Book which was settling in their common folk must not settle; rather, they should keep it in the same category in which the speech of great poets or powerful orators is kept. Their main dispute was with the claim of the Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) mission being the revelation of God. In refuting this, they said: the claim of this Quran being divine revelation is completely a lie. This is no divine revelation; rather, (seeking refuge in Allah) this is the speech fabricated by Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself, which he falsely attributes to Allah to establish his authority over us.

"Wa-a'anahu 'alayhi qawmun akharoon"—to make their statement argued, they added another lie: that in the composition of this Book, some other minds are also partners with Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The need for this addition must have been felt because in the Quran, references to the stories and teachings of past prophets were present, to know which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had no apparent medium. Regarding them, this question arose: if this is no divine revelation, how did the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) know these things, and that too with such detail and correctness that even the claimants of faith in the past scrolls did not know with such detail and correctness! In answer to this question, they added this lie too: some other hands are also partners in this conspiracy. The ambiguity present in this statement of the opponents is because: when the accusation is a lie, only the style of ambiguity is appropriate for it. If the style of specification is adopted for it, its secret is exposed. To whom did this pointer of the opponents refer? In answer to this question, our commentators have quoted various statements. In our view, it is better for this ambiguity to remain ambiguous, because the purpose of the accusers was merely to make an accusation, not to actually trace any person or persons. The Quraysh tried to give their common folk this impression through this ambiguous accusation: do not consider the matters and wisdoms in the style of heavenly Books in this Quran as the result of revelation; rather, some People of the Book or educated individuals of other nations who have become companions of Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) make such things for him, and he presents them before us in his eloquent words, saying: "The angel of revelation brought them upon him." Take a look at what we have written under verse 103 of Surah An-Nahl, too.

Meaning of "Zulm" and "Zoor":
"Faqad ja'oo dulman wa-zooran"—"ja'oo" is used here in the meaning of committing, and this use is well-known. By "dulm" is meant their Shirk mentioned in the above verse, and "zoor" points to their statement quoted regarding the Quran. He said: "These people committed the great wrong of Shirk, and along with it, a very great lie in opposing the Quran." Saying this, the matter is concluded. No pointer is made to its result, which means: those who have committed such great crimes with such defiance—estimate what end they are worthy of!

The correct meaning of "ikttataba fulan" in the language is: "sa'ala an yuktaba lahu" (he requested that it be written down for him).

After "tumla", the preposition "'ala" is a clue that this contains the meaning of "tulqa 'alayhi" (cast upon him) or "tuqrara 'alayhi" (read out to him). For this reason, its meaning can also be that they are written down for him, and also that they are taught to him in writing. Here, it is in the second meaning, because the Quraysh knew that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was unlettered; he did not know how to write.

The attempt of the Quraysh to throw dust on the sun:
This is the further explanation of the above accusation: they say: "What is in this Quran? Merely tales of the past which have been repeated in it! This person requested some people to write them down for him, so they write them down for him morning and afternoon, and this person presents their written things to us as divine revelation." The meaning is: neither is there any such wisdom in this Book to be awed by, nor is this claim of its presenter correct that this is heavenly revelation which is sent down upon him; rather, there are some false and true stories of the past nations and past prophets which this person requested other people to write down for him. In this manner, they tried to erase the effect of the Quran's wisdom and also to make its claim of being heavenly revelation, but as is obvious, this attempt of theirs was equivalent to throwing dust on the sun. The history the Quran presented with such wisdom fell directly on the pride of the arrogant of Quraysh, and inside its mirror, they saw their future highly terrifying; for this reason, they angrily called it the tales of the past, but by calling the reality a tale, neither can one console one's own heart nor can one deceive others. If a Salman the Persian, an Abu Fakiha the Roman, or some People of the Book together, according to the assumption of the Quraysh, could compose a miraculous Book like the Quran, the Quraysh had thousands of Arabs, non-Arabs, and millions of People of the Book—after all, why did they not prepare and present a Quran by their help, so that the claim of its being a miracle would be refuted! But when they could not present any parallel to it despite the continuous challenge of the Quran, who could believe their absurd statement: this great Book is the invention of some non-Arabs or some People of the Book? Since this statement was obviously absurd, the Quran did not feel the need to refute it. In the subsequent verse, only the actual reality is expressed, which contains also a subtle sarcasm on the absurdity of these busybodies.

Subtle sarcasm and warning to the objectors:
He said: "Tell these people: He who has sent down this Book is He who is well-aware of all the secrets and all the mysteries of the heavens and the earth. He is not in need of any Arab or non-Arab's help for its composition. No matter how great a wise and learned person may be, he can obtain insight and guidance from this Book, but let alone composing a book like this, he cannot even add to its knowledge and wisdom." This reality is clear from every single line of this Book: He who sent it down is He who is aware of all the secrets of this universe and of its apparent, hidden, beginning, and end; consequently, He has placed the lessons of the past, clarified the responsibilities of the present, and unveiled the results of the future.

"Innahu kana ghafooran raheeman" (Indeed, He is Forgiving and Merciful)—meaning, because He is Forgiving and Merciful, He sent down this Book so that those who want to make repentance and reform may become worthy of His mercy. The meaning is: if He wanted, He could have sent down the punishment too. He is well-aware of the attitude of the enemies of truth, but He, because of His mercy, does not make haste in giving punishment; rather, He wants people to adopt the path of guidance to be worthy of His forgiveness. In this is also a pointer to the fact: He is well-aware of all situations and matters. If people did not adopt the correct way, the end they have to face will also come!

Verses 7-8:
This is the reference to some other objections of the opponents.

Some other objections of the objectors to the Messenger:
In the style of "Ma li-hadha ar-rasooli" (What is with this messenger?), there is both wonder and mockery. That is, they say: "He is a strange messenger, claiming to be God's messenger, while his state is: like us, he too is in need of eating and drinking, and to provide his needs, like other people, he too goes about in the markets." The meaning is: if God wanted to send a messenger, would He make a human being like ourselves a messenger? After all, He has an army of angels—why did He not send one of them as a messenger.

"Lawla unzila ilayhi malakun fayakoona ma'ahu nadheeran"—meaning: "If He had to make some human being a messenger, at least this should have been the case: there should have been some angel with him to make proclamation: 'O people, he is the Messenger of Allah; fear what he is warning you of,' or some treasury should have been sent down upon him from the sky, or he should have possessed some magnificent garden from which he would have obtained his living, and he would not have had to go about wearing out his shoes in the markets like common men!" The meaning is: when none of these things is there, how can we accept him as God's messenger!

"Wa qala ad-dalimoona in tattabi'oona illa rajulan masdooran"—meaning, after the aforementioned objections, these wrongdoers, addressing the Muslims, say: "If you have accepted a person as a messenger, it means you have become followers of a person on whom someone has worked magic to lose his intellect, because of which he has begun to utter confused words."

The loss of intellect of the objectors:
This verse has passed exactly in Surah Al-Isra too. See verse 48. There we have explained: the idiom of "darb al-mathal" (striking a comparison), in the manner it comes for describing some parable or saying some wise word, comes also for making an objection or criticizing someone. In the meaning of false objection, this word will come forward in verse 32 of this Surah too. Here, it is in the meaning of criticism or objection. After quoting the aforementioned absurdities of the opponents, addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), He said: "See, what kind of criticisms they are trying to make against you, but no word is seen being made; so, whatever comes to whose mouth, he utters it."

"Fadalloo fala yastatee'oona sabeelan"—meaning, they are completely lost in the madness of opposition. They are finding no path of objection, nor will they be able to. They are merely trying to let out the steam of their heart, but in this manner, how long will they be able to deny the reality! It should be kept in mind: when any person knowingly opposes some sct-y truth, he demonstrates the same kind of loss of intellect which is present inside these objections.

The upcoming theme... Verses 10-34:
Forward, He has answered the aforementioned objections, shed light on their actual motives, and also pointed to that destructive end which is going to come before this blind and deaf opposition. Within this connection, some new objections have also come under discussion, and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions are directed to ignore all these absurdities with patience and steadfastness. In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

The answer to the objections:
In the manner the transition to the objections was raised with the word "Tabarak" (Blessed), similarly the transition to the answer is also raised with this word, and first of all, He took that objection of the opponents which is quoted last in verse 8. He said: "The Entity of God is highly generous and possesses great favor and kindness. In His presence is no lack of gardens, nor of treasuries." "Jannat" refers to a garden. "If God wills, He can make for you many gardens beneath which rivers flow and prepare many palaces for you, and all this is going to be obtained by you one day." But their gazes are limited only to the life of this world; they possess no conception of the life of the Hereafter, which is the actual life; for this reason, they are proud of their worldly means and resources and are mocking your lack of means, whereas this world is the home of trial (dar al-imtihan), not the home of reward (dar al-in'am). The poverty and wealth here are for trial. The home of reward is the Hereafter—whoever succeeds there is successful forever, and whoever is unsuccessful there is unsuccessful forever, and the success of there is for those who fear God in this world.

This points to the actual cause of their delusion: they are making their worldly successes the proof of their being on the right because they are deniers of the Resurrection, whereas they do not know: those who are deniers of the Resurrection—We have prepared Hell for them, even though they may possess how many lands, how many gardens, and how many mansions in this world!

Picture of Hell and the people of Hell:
"Taghayyud" means to boil with anger, and "zafeer" means to cry out and shriek. This is the attribute of that Hell which Allah has prepared for these wrongdoers. He said: "She is already bapph-ree-ed with anger in taking revenge from them, and on seeing them from far, she will shriek in the manner a hungry lion shrieks."

This is the picture of their punishment of Hell: He said: "When they are thrown, bound, into some highly narrow place of it, they will call upon their death there." That is: first, the place is narrow, and then in it too, they will be thrown bound and chained. In another place, He said: (Indeed, it is closed in upon them, in extended columns) [Al-Humazah: 8-9]—meaning, it will be closed over them and they will be bound with long columns inside it. In another place, the mention of chains (salasil) and collars (aghlal) has also entered.

"Da'aw thubooran"—"thuboor" means death and destruction; i.e., at that time, they will cry out for death, because the only form of release from this punishment which they will see will be: how death should come to put an end to their life, but death will not come there. In another place, there is the specification: death will be coming upon them from every side, but they will not die; rather, they will remain involved in the grief of "he will neither die in it nor live" [Ta-Ha: 74].

On that day, it will be said to them: "Do not cry for only one death today, but cry for many deaths." That is: "Today, countless new calamities will break upon you, and on every calamity you have to cry in this manner, but all this crying of yours will be completely useless." This can be in words—meaning, it will be said to them by Allah through the keepers of Hell—and it can be the expression of the state's situation. The explanation of this style has passed in places.

Verses 15-16:
Picture of Paradise and the people of Paradise:
Now, this is the mention of the actual purpose for which all this detail of Hell was narrated. Addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), He said: "Ask those who are gibing you that you should possess some garden: is this Hell better which is prepared for them, or that Paradise of eternity which Allah has promised to His God-fearing servants?" That is: "Your gazes are limited only to the life of this world; for this reason, you are proud of your gardens and palaces and are gibing the believers for their lack of means. If you had the knowledge of the Hereafter and knew what punishment is prepared there for you, and what eternal kingdom is prepared for the believers, you would envy these poor believers and beat your heads over your wretchedness of actions, but you have been made blind by the deception of this world in such a manner that you cannot see beyond your nose." Regarding this Paradise, four things have been stated here:

One: this will be granted to the believers in reward and exchange for their actions. Allah will assure them: "You have obtained this through your effort and action, and you are fully worthy of it." 
Second: this will be their eternal staying-place; they will never have any fear of being deprived of it.
Third: in it, they will obtain whatever they intend, and will obtain forever.
Fourth: the promise of this Paradise has been made by Allah as a binding promise, and He has made His fulfillment of it obligatory upon Himself, making Himself responsible and answerable for it before His servants.

Verses 17-18:
In verses 2-3 above, those partners have been mentioned on whose trust these polytheists, being indifferent to the Hereafter, were mocking the Quran and the Messenger. Here, keeping them in view, He said: "On the Day of Resurrection, Allah will gather them along with those deities whom they worship besides Allah, and will ask them: 'Did you mislead My these servants, or did they lose the path themselves?' They will answer: 'Glory are You! How was it worthy for us to take besides You any protector or helper, or direct others to do so! The actual thing is: You granted the abundance of worldly living to them and their ancestors, in which many of their generations passed, because of which they assumed they were created for luxury, they will live in it and die in it, being its birth-rightful owners. They assumed: Resurrection first of all is no thing, and if hypothetically it exists, in the manner we are superior to all in this world, we will be superior to all in the Hereafter too. This delusion of theirs kept them turned away from the path of truth, and those who reminded them of the actual reality—they denied them on the strength of their worldly superiority, becoming a destroyed people.'"

From this answer, it is completely clear: this will be the answer of those prophets, righteous, and angels who were worshipped. As for those imaginary idols and stones of the polytheists which have no reality—although they too will be gathered, as clear from the generality of "ma ya'budoon" (what they worship), yet what question of questioning them! However, in other places in the Quran, there is the specification: they too will be thrown into Hell along with their worshippers, so that those wretched who worshipped them may clearly see what has happened to those before whom they bowed down all their life!

The answer of the prophets and righteous quoted here is exactly the same answer quoted from the tongue of Jesus in verses 115-118 of Surah Al-Ma'idah; take a look at it too.

The actual cause of denial:
In "Walakin matta'tahum wa-aba'ahum hatta nasoo adh-dhikra", the same is said as is stated in Surah Al-Hadid, verse 16: (and a long period passed over them, so their hearts hardened). In the verse, by "dhikr" is meant that reminder which was given to them repeatedly to remember the Hereafter. This theme is stated in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 44, and Surah Al-Qasas, verse 45 too. If further explanation is required, take a look at them too.

"Qawman booran"—"booran" is used equally for singular, plural, masculine, and feminine. Its meaning is barren land, and also corrupt and useless person. Here, it is used as an attribute of the nation, meaning a destroyed nation.

An eloquent style:
Here, the future matter is put in the past form in accordance with the demand of eloquence. He said: "These people—they have indeed denied your word which you said! Your claim indeed was: they commanded you to their worship, and you are acting on those things only in accordance with their direction, but their statement is: let alone commanding anyone to their worship, they do not tolerate even for a single moment that anyone make them their protector and helper."

Sorrow and the punishment of the great wrong:
"Fama tastatee'oona sarfan wa-la nasran..."—meaning, after this final completion of proof and cutting of excuses, no path of escape will be left for you. Neither will you be able to ward off the punishment from yourselves, nor will you be able to help yourselves or anyone else; and whosoever among you commits the wrong of Shirk, We will make him taste a great punishment. It should be clear: the punishment of Shirk is a great punishment because, in the presence of Allah, Shirk holds the status of a great wrong.

All messengers were human beings:
In this verse, although the address is apparently to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the words of the verse themselves are proof that the face of speech is toward the common people; consequently, both singular and plural pronouns and verbs are used in it. This is the answer to that objection of the opponents which is quoted above in verse 7. He said: "If you are a human being, eating food, and also having to go to the markets for purchase and sale, none of these things is contrary to the office of messengership. All those messengers whom We sent before you, without exception, ate food and went about in the markets like common men." This answer of the Quran is completely clear, because these objectors accepted Hazrat Ibrahim and Hazrat Ismail (peace be upon them) not only as prophets and messengers but also took great pride in being their offspring and the heirs of their religion, yet regarding none of them was their claim that they were superhuman; rather, they accepted them as human beings. Similarly, the children of Israel were not believers in any of their prophets being superhuman. Consequently, in another place, the Quran has said to these objectors of Quraysh: "If you are unaware of the history of prophethood and messengership, ask those People of the Book who are acquainted with the history of prophets and messengers: did their prophets eat food or not, and did they face the need to go about in the markets or not?" Although the Christians, since the time of Paul, tried hard to make Jesus superhuman, they too cannot deny his and his mother's eating of food, because in the Gospels, this is present with great clarity. Consequently, the Quran has presented this also as proof against their claim of divinity: (They both used to eat food) [Al-Ma'idah: 75].

A trial for the prophets and their nations:
"Wa ja'alna ba'dakum liba'din fitnatan"—this exact same theme has passed in Surah Al-An'am, verse 53. There, He said: (And thus We try some of them by others that they [i.e., the wealthy] might say, "Are these the ones whom Allah has favored among us?"). That is: the poverty of the Muslims has become a trial for these disbelievers. They say: "When we are the heirs and owners of all blessings and wealth, this religion, if it were some divine religion, how could it be given to these poor Muslims? It would have been given to us; and if Allah had to make a human being a prophet, He would have made some chief of Ta'if or Makkah, not a poor man like Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)!" Similarly, He said here: "We have made some of you a trial for others." That is: your poverty has become a veil for them in the path of accepting the truth. The right was: on receiving the blessings, they should have become grateful servants of God, but these blessings became a cause of arrogance for them, and in the intoxication of this arrogance, instead of reflecting over your call and choosing it, they made you the target of objections and criticisms; and this is a trial of your patience from Allah.

An eloquent style of Arabic:
"Atasbiroon"—this is addressing the Muslims: "They indeed failed completely in the trial of their gratitude; now, tell: are you going to remain steadfast against their mockery, enmity, and opposition?" Those who possess taste for the Arabic language know: when the meaning of encouragement and motivation is to be created in the command, it enters in the form of a predicate, and if the letter of interrogation comes upon it, even more force is born. Under "Hal antum mujtami'oon" [Ash-Shu'ara: 39], further explanation of this style will come, and behind too, some excellent examples of this have passed. This has urged the Muslims: "If your opponents failed in their trial, leave them to their state; before you is the stage of the trial of patience—advance with determination and courage to succeed in it."

"Wa kana rabbuka baseeran"—meaning, your Lord is supervising your state. Have full trust in His help. If you remain steadfast, Allah will reach you to the destination against all these oppositions.

In this verse, Allah has attributed the trial to Himself, which is a pointer to that Divine Sunnah which He has established in this world for His servants' trial. We are explaining this Divine Sunnah in places.

Verses 21-22:
A blow to the arrogance of the arrogant:
By "alladhina la yarjoona liqa'ana" (those who do not expect the meeting with Us), that group of the arrogant is expressed by the Quran which was completely carefree and fearless of the presence before God. The ignorance, pride, and arrogance of this group was far greater than of other disbelievers; for this reason, their mention is made not by the word "alladhina kafaroo" but with such an attribute which fully exposes their arrogance. The demand of this group was: "If the angel of God comes to Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), as is his claim, why do the angels not come to us, or why does it not happen that God Himself appears without any veil before us and we see Him?" This demand, as is obvious, could be made by those who were completely rebellious, proud, and of extreme foolishness; for this reason, instead of answering their foolish statement, He struck a blow at this arrogance of theirs. He said: "These people showed great pride in rising with the demand to see God and the angels. They do not estimate what seeing God and these angels is! Let alone God, when that day comes on which they see the angels, that will be no glad tiding-giving day for them; rather, on that day, they will cry out: 'Refuge! Refuge!'" Forward, further detail of the terror of that day is coming.

Meaning of "Hijran Mahjooran":
One meaning of "hijran mahjooran" is a strict veil and cover. In this meaning, this word will come forward in verse 53 of this Surah. Second: this is among the words of seeking refuge (isti'adhah), and the opinion of Sibawayh is: when it comes in this meaning, it is used exactly in this form and is in the accusative by an omitted verb, in the manner "ma'adh Allah" is used.

The nonentity of the actions of the arrogant:
He said: "Those services and those achievements on which these people are highly proud, and because of which they assume they are worthy of being called the heroes of the country and nation in this world, and if the Hereafter exists, they will obtain very great statuses there—We will turn all those actions of theirs into scattered dust. This is because in Our presence, that action is of no value which is not done solely for Our pleasure." From the style of expression "qadimna ila ma 'amiloo" (We will proceed to what they did), the point also emerges: "We Ourselves will advance to reject their every action, whether that action be great or small." This is proof of the extreme humiliation of their actions from Allah.

In contrast to them, the state of the people of Paradise is described: those whom they looked at with extreme contempt in this world will be, on that day, in the best staying-place and the highest resting-place. "Maqeel" means the place of the midday nap (qayloolah), but in its common use, it comes in the meaning of resting-place and home of luxury. Imru' al-Qays's hemistich is: (So let us make merry in the place of luxury, which is safe from every ill fortune). "Khayr" (better) and "ahsan" (best) here are not in the meaning of comparison; rather, they are in the meaning of the most beautiful and the best. We have explained this in another place.

Verses 25-26:
The manifestation of the angels on the Day of Resurrection:
This too is a part of the answer given to those demanding to see the angels. He said: "These people should conceptualize that day when the sky will split and a cloud will appear from it, and from inside it, the angels, row after row, will be sent down one after another." He said: "On that day, no one's power will run. Those deities on whom these people have great pride and reliance will also not be of any use to them. The true sovereignty on that day will belong only to the Merciful God, and for the disbelievers, this day will be highly difficult!" It should be kept in mind: the true choice and the true sovereignty, though always God's, is hidden under a veil in this world; for this reason, the fools fall into a delusion and begin to beat the drum of their own sovereignty; but on the Day of Resurrection, it will become clearly illuminated to every person: the entire force and choice belongs to Allah alone— no other has any choice.

Verses 27-29:
The regret of the deniers of the Messenger on the Day of Resurrection:
Biting the hands is an expression for showing regret and remorse, and this expression is present in our language too. By "dalim" (wrongdoer) here is meant the wrongdoer to his own soul—meaning, every person who opposed the Messenger. He said: "These people should conceptualize that day when every person who today is blind and deaf in opposing the Messenger, and in this manner doing wrong to his own soul, will bite his hands with extreme regret, saying: 'O, would that I had chosen the path of Allah's Messenger with him! And, would that I had not taken such a one as a friend, who turned me away from the reminder after it had come to me!'"

In "adallanee" (he misled me), the preposition "Min" is a clue that this contains the meaning of turning away. That is: "He, through those misleading things he said against the Messenger and the Book of Allah, misled me and turned me away from the path shown by the Messenger." The reference to these things is present in verses 4, 11, 21, 32, and 41-42.

Within "ba'da idh ja'atnee" (after it had come to me), there is the confession of one's own wretchedness: "If the guidance of Allah had not come to me, I could have presented some excuse, but now what excuse can I present when the proof was completed upon me."

"Wa-kana ash-shaytanu lil-insani khadhoolan"—"khadhool" means one who deserts in time of need, unfaithful, and betrayer.

In my view, this is not a part of the statement of those expressing regret, as people have commonly assumed; rather, this is a timely reminder and warning from Allah on their statement: "Satan is in this manner a betrayer and deserter in time of need." The word "Satan" here is in the meaning of genus. By it are meant the devils of both jinn and humans. The meaning is: in the world, these insolent people are indeed companions, leaders, and followers to each other, being hand and support in spreading every misguidance and suppressing the truth, but when the stage of the Hereafter presents itself, they will curse each other. The followers will say: "Such a one misled us; if he had not deceived us, we would have been on the straight path." And the leaders will answer: "You yourselves were wretched to follow us; we only made you what we ourselves were."

The complaint of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) against his nation on the Day of Resurrection:
By "ar-rasool" (the Messenger) is meant the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The meaning is: on one hand, those who denied the Quran will beat their heads over their own wretchedness; on the other hand, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) too will make a complaint against his nation before his Lord: "O Lord! I presented Your Book, with complete sympathy, before my these people, but they did not appreciate it in the least; rather, with extreme disregard, they rejected it." This complaint of the Prophet will be the final nail in the coffin of these wretched ones, after which there will remain no room for them to open their mouths. This matter has passed in detail in Surah Al-Ma'idah: on the Day of Resurrection, the prophets will give testimony regarding their nations.

Comfort to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him):
This is to comfort the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "This opposition these wretched ones are committing with you is nothing new in the history of the prophets. The criminals of every era have in this manner opposed their respective messengers." The word "criminals" has passed in verse 24 above. This word is used for those misleading leaders whose attitude is under discussion in this Surah. He said: "These very criminals are responsible for all those corruptions which the Prophet wants to reform, and their interest is associated with all these corruptions; for this reason, they do not tolerate the call of the Prophet, but rise with all their weapons for its opposition; but remain assured, your Lord is sufficient to guide and help you in every difficulty."

Since this thing happens in accordance with the Divine Sunnah, because in this world Allah has given respite to the people of falsehood to some extent, for this reason, He has attributed it to Himself. The explanation of this Divine Sunnah has passed in places. In verse 112 of Surah Al-An'am, this theme has passed with more clarity. Those seeking detail may take a look at it.

The reason why the Quran was not sent down all at once:
This is the fourth objection of the objectors quoted. They also spread this idea among the people against the Quran: "If this is a Book sent down by God, why was it not sent down all at once, as God does not need to think and plan to send it down slowly?" They made this objection to strengthen their same propaganda mentioned above: "Some other people are also partners in the composition of this Book. Consequently, as much as they prepare, they teach this person (Muhammad [peace and blessings of Allah be upon him]) and he recites it to us as divine revelation." In answer to this, He said: "We have sent it down in this manner so that in this manner We may strengthen your heart, and We have sent it down with a specific gradual development and care so that people may learn it." The meaning is: We could have sent down the entire Book all at once, but the question was of your endurance and the people's teaching and training. If this entire Book had been sent down all at once, neither you could have lifted this burden, nor the completion of proof could have been done upon people on a correct basis; for this reason, Allah sent it down slowly so that you too may bear it and people too may learn it if they want to. A teacher, if he wants, can read out the entire book in a single sitting to the students, but can the students also preserve the book in this manner!

"Wa rattalnahu tarteelan"—"tarteel" means to read and recite with care and distinctness. This is conjoined to that verb which is assumed as omitted after "kadhalika". This is stated here by way of showing favor: "This is Our extreme kindness upon people that We did not hand over the entire Book all at once, but are reading and reciting it with care and gradual development, lesson by lesson." The meaning is: this thing is to be appreciated and thanked for, rather than being made an objection; but these wrongdoers made this thing also a cause of objection.

None of the heavenly scrolls was sent down all at once:
It should be kept in mind: none of the heavenly scrolls was sent down all at once. Those who have assumed so, their assumption is based on unfamiliarity with those scrolls. The Torah also was not sent down all at once. All at once were sent down only its Ten Commandments, which were given to Hazrat Musa written on tablets. But the Torah is not comprised of the Ten Commandments alone; in it are all the commands and laws of the Mosaic Shariah, which were sent down stage by stage during the entire life of Hazrat Musa. Among them, many commands are such that regarding them, in both the Quran and the Torah, this specification is present: they were sent down before the migration of Hazrat Musa, many commands were sent down after crossing the river, or during the life of the Wilderness of Sinai. Similarly, the Gospel is entirely comprised of the wise advices of our master Jesus, which were sent down upon him on various occasions according to the requirements of situations, and he warned his nation by them. This same is the state of the Psalms. It is comprised of the systematic prayers and received communications of Hazrat Dawud, which were inspired to him from time to time. In short, this assumption is completely baseless: any of these Books was sent down all at once. After all, how could this be! The prophets (peace be upon them) were no authors to write entire books and hand them over to people all at once. They were callers, teachers, and purifiers—they had to treat and purify an entire sick nation. The natural and cosmic requirement of their office was: they should take steps on this path of reform with gradual development, and at every step, obtain guidance from Allah according to the need. As far as I know, even the scholars of the People of the Book do not claim that any of their books was sent down all at once, and if anyone is, his claim is contrary to the clear specifications of his book. This statement our commentators write: "the Torah, Psalms, and Gospel were sent down all at once," is not correct.

Under verse 9, we have explained: the idiom of "darb al-mathal" (striking a comparison) comes also for objection and criticism. Here, the context is proof: it is in the meaning of objection, and in the verse, the word "haqq" (truth) is used in contrast to it, from which the point emerged: by it is meant false objection here.

Comfort to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him):
After giving highly clear and heart-settling answers to all the objections of the objectors, this is to comfort the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Remain assured. Whatever false objection they raise against you, the responsibility to answer it is upon Us. We will send down its correct answer and its best explanation and interpretation upon you." The need for this comfort was: the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) gave His mission and invitation of the Quran to the people by the command of God—no slight part of His own desire was in it. Because of this claim and invitation of yours, the entire nation became your enemy, and from every side, the shower of objections began. If Allah had not assumed the responsibility to defend against all these attacks of the opponents, how could you have closed the mouths of all creation on your own.

The actual destination of the misguidance of the astray:
After "yuhsharoon" (they will be gathered), the preposition "'Ala" is a clue that this contains the meaning of "yus-haboon" (they will be dragged). In another place, its clarification has also been done: (The Day they are dragged into the Fire on their faces) [Al-Qamar: 48].

This verse is in contrast to verse 24 above. In that, the glad tiding of a good end was given to the people of Paradise; in this, the end of the people of Hell is mentioned: those who will be dragged on their faces toward Hell will be in the worst place and the most astray from the path.

Within "adallu sabeelan" (most astray from the path), pointer is made to the reality: in this world, the astray do not have the estimation of the actual destination of their misguidance; for this reason, they do not realize where the path they are walking on is taking them. In the Hereafter, when the actual destination of their misguidance—Hell—appears, only then they will estimate: from where they started and where they reached!

The upcoming theme... Verses 35-44:
Forward is a brief reference to Hazrat Musa, Hazrat Nuh, and those nations between them in whom messengers were sent. The purpose of this reference is to comfort the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and to warn his nation: Allah informed those nations too of His teachings and directions, but they, instead of appreciating them, mocked those who presented those teachings, whose result ultimately was: they faced a highly destructive end. In this Surah, there is only a pointer to the events. Forward in the next Surah, which is its companion, the detail of these events will come. The order of the events is ascending and also descending. First, He mentioned Hazrat Musa, who is the most majestic possessor of Book and Shariah prophet and messenger of the Mosaic chain. After that, He mentioned Hazrat Nuh, from whom, after Hazrat Adam, the chain of messengership begins. This is the pointer from below to above. Then, He mentioned the other nations of the middle, and in their mention, the order is descending—meaning, from above to below. Recite the verses.

Simultaneous mention of Hazrat Haroon alongside Hazrat Musa in terms of the completion of proof:
Reference to Hazrat Haroon alongside Hazrat Musa as his helper, which enters repeatedly, is to highlight that aspect of favor and completion of proof for which Allah adopted this method: He appointed another prophet alongside a messenger for his support. In another place, the detail has passed: in terms of expression, Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) felt some lack inside himself; for this reason, he prayed: let Hazrat Haroon be made his partner in the work so that with the help of his eloquence and speech, he may preach the truth with full strength and confidence, so that no lack remains in performing the duty from the aspect of calling and completion of proof. Consequently, Allah accepted his prayer, and both these prophets called the Egyptians simultaneously, but they did not appreciate this favor and care of Allah.

Since here is a brief pointer, the entire matter is summarized in two phrases. The meaning is: We sent both Our messengers with Our signs to those people, but they denied all those signs, calling them magic, whose punishment ultimately was: We destroyed them completely. In this is comfort for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): those whose hearts are defiled do not accept guidance under any circumstances, to the extent that they deny two messengers and all their miracles simultaneously; and in this, alongside, is a warning to the Quraysh: if they too follow the path of Pharaoh and his nation, there is no reason why their end should be different from theirs!

Denial of one messenger is denial of all messengers:
Although the people of Noah denied only one messenger—Hazrat Nuh—the plural word "ar-rusul" (the messengers) is used here. Its cause in our view is what we have explained under verse 59 of Surah Hud: the message of all messengers is one and their Sender is also One; for this reason, the denial of one is the denial of all. By this way of expression, the purpose is to direct attention to the severity of this crime: let those who deny some messenger think: where does this matter reach.

"Wa ja'alnahum linnasi ayatan" (And We made them a sign for mankind)—meaning, We made their end a lesson for those after them, so that they may obtain a lesson: those who deny the messengers—ultimately their end is this.

"Wa-a'tadna lid-dalimeena 'adhaban aleeman" (And We have prepared for the wrongdoers a painful punishment)—meaning, the punishment of this kind of wrongdoers does not conclude on the punishment of this world alone; rather, We have prepared a painful punishment for them in the Hereafter too.

Here, based on context, the verb "ahlakna" (We destroyed) is assumed as omitted.

Who were the Companions of the Rass (Ashab ar-Rass)?
In the research on the Companions of the Rass, I am sorry that success could not be achieved yet. Ibn Jarir has mentioned various names, but on none of them is he himself content. At the end of the discussion, he has clarified: regarding none of them can anything be said with certainty.

The author of 'Kashshaf' has pointed also to the nation of Shu'ayb along with other names, and has kept this prior, but this is not correct under any circumstances, because in Surah Qaf, the Companions of the Rass and the Companions of the Thicket (Ashab al-Aykah) have entered as two independent nations, and by the Companions of the Thicket, in accordance with the specification of the Quran itself, as will be explained in the next Surah, the people of Midian—meaning the nation of Hazrat Shu'ayb—are meant. If the Companions of the Rass meant the nation of Shu'ayb, what was the context of mentioning the Companions of the Thicket alongside them!

Maulana Syed Sulaiman Nadvi, in 'Ardu al-Quran', has meant Qaydar from among the twelve chains of the Ismaili tribes, but has also admitted: its situations are completely unknown. Ignoring the unknown situations, among the children of Ismail, the mission of no messenger is proved except of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and here the mention of the Companions of the Rass has entered in such a context from which it is clear: a messenger was sent to them and they denied him, in the manner of 'Ad and Thamud.

In any case, saying anything with certainty regarding them is difficult. Only this is known: this is a reference to some tribe of the desert tribes of Arabia, to whom Allah sent His messenger, but it denied him and became worthy of punishment under the Divine Sunnah. Among the poets of ignorance, Zuhair has mentioned the valley of Rass: (And they were of the valley of Rass like the mouth to the hand).

The inability of history:
(And many generations between them.) In the Quran, in places, the mention is present: the prophets and messengers are not only those mentioned in the Quran; rather, there are others besides them, whose knowledge is with Allah alone. Those who want to see every thing in the light of history are unaware of the inability of history. The reach of history is very limited, and its major part is comprised of all kinds of material. With the affairs of this world, the most well-aware is its Creator; for this reason, the primary reliance must be on the Book of Allah, not on the books of history. If the Quran has mentioned some historical event, this is a blessed addition to our knowledge. If history too supports it, then excellent; if not, attribute it to the inability of history.

Meaning of "Darb al-Amthal":
"Darb al-amthal" here is in the meaning of clarifying realities with complete details and all requirements of reminder and warning. When some matter is to be fully established in the mind of someone and he is to be fully informed of its consequences, parables and historical events and situations which picture the realities are highly effective. The prophets (peace be upon them), since they came to complete the proof upon people, did not content themselves merely with describing principles; rather, they also made them argued and proved through parables and historical events. Consequently, you see in the Quran: every reality is clearly explained through parables and events in such a manner that except an obstinate, no one can deny them. This same method was adopted by all messengers. After this too, those who did not understand, as is obvious, were not people to understand by intellect and heart, but were those to believe only after seeing with their eyes. Regarding such, the Divine Sunnah, as we have been explaining, is: they are destroyed by that divine wrath which they denied and whose seeing with their eyes they demanded. Consequently, He said: We explained to all those nations well, but when they refused to understand, We destroyed them completely. The meaning is: this same end is going to be of your nation too if it does not appreciate the Quran.

Insight inside eyes is born from the concept of the Hereafter:
This points to the town of the people of Lot. By "umtirat matra as-saw'i" (rained upon with an evil rain) is meant that punishment which came upon them. The nature of this punishment we have explained in its place. If its complete research is required, read the commentary of Surah Adh-Dhariyat in 'Majmu'ah Tafaseer Farahi'.

Pointing to the Quraysh, He said: if they are far or unfamiliar with the affairs of other nations, are they unfamiliar with the signs of this nation too which became the target of Our highly terrifying punishment! On this town, they pass day and night in their commercial journeys! Do they not see its signs too! They see, but since they do not believe in being raised again after death, they are blind despite seeing everything. It should be kept in mind: the commercial caravans of the Quraysh passed on the ruins of the towns of the people of Lot. The explanation of this we have done in its place.

In this verse, this reality was clarified: inside the eyes of man, insight is born from the concept of the Hereafter. If this is not there, a person apparently looks, but sees nothing.

Verses 41-42:
That is, Allah indeed favored them by clarifying the realities of this world and the Hereafter through you and the Quran, but the state of their deprivation and wretchedness is: whenever they see you, they mock you, saying: "Is this the one whom Allah has sent as a messenger! He would have indeed turned us away from our deities if we had not remained firm on their worship!" He said: "Today, they are highly proud of remaining firm on their foolishness, but tomorrow, when they deal with the punishment as a result of this ignorance of theirs, only then they will estimate: our Prophet did not want to mislead them, but guided them toward the Straight Path, leaving which they reached where they reached!" Within "man adallu sabeelan" (who is most astray from the path), pointer is made to that reality which we have explained under verse 34 above: the astray do not have the estimation of their misguidance until its end appears. They are slaves to their desires, and those desires make them blind in such a manner that no reminder or warning is effective upon them.

Verses 43-44:
Slaves to desires are worse than beasts:
On the style of "Ara'ayta..." (Have you seen?), we have discussed in its place. This is to comfort the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Those who have suspended their intellect to hand over their rein to the desires of their soul—how can you be responsible for their guidance and reform! Inside man, the lamp of guidance is the intellect, not the desires of the soul; so, those who extinguish this lamp to become worshippers of their desires, who has the power to show them the path!" We have explained in another place: all desires are blind. They only want to fulfill their demand. They have no concern with what is truth and what is falsehood, what is good and what is evil; so, the person who follows them, and that too in such a manner as though they are his deities, falls into the trap of Satan in such a manner that escaping from it becomes impossible for him.

"Am tahsabu anna..."—meaning: "You (address to the Prophet) who are spending your night and day in the worry of their reform in this manner—do you expect that some capability of hearing and understanding is still remaining in them? If this is your assumption, it is not correct. They have lost all their capability of hearing and understanding, becoming like beasts, rather worse than beasts."

We have explained in another place: the comparison of the worshippers of desires, who have suspended their intellect, with beasts is no exaggeration in style, but the statement of reality. Beasts, under all circumstances, remain on that instinct on which Allah created them. They do not deviate from their instinct in the pursuit of their desires. But man, when he becomes a slave to his desires, breaks all limits of instinct and nature, becoming worse than beasts.

The upcoming theme... Verses 45-62:
In the previous verses, you saw that comfort was given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in the light of the history's testimonies. Now forward, attention is directed toward some signs of the horizons and the universe, which confirm and support all those matters you presented before your nation. Pointing to these signs, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is also directed: those arguments and proofs with which you must be armed to perform your duty are explained in various styles in the Quran, so wage Jihad through this Book alone to complete the proof of Allah upon these people, and do not care in the least about their objections and demands which they make to corner you or merely to maintain their prestige. In this light, recite the upcoming verses.

Verses 45-46:
Bringing the night and the day is in the power of God alone:
"Alam tara..." is addressing every single individual here. By "zill" (shadow/canopy) here is meant the shadow of the night, which Allah spreads over the entire world, and if He were to impose it permanently, no one has the power to remove it. This is His majesty and power that He spreads this shadow every day and sems-ats it every day. For the survival of this world, just as its spreading is necessary, its sems-at-ing is also necessary, and in both these matters, except Allah without partners, no one has any choice. He alone makes His sun, which rises every day, as the guide inside this darkness to remove it slowly. This same theme has entered in Surah Al-Qasas thus:
(Say, "Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the night continuous until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you light? Then will you not hear?" Say, "Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the day continuous until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you night in which you find rest? Then will you not see?" And out of His mercy He made for you the night and the day that you may rest therein and to seek of His bounty and that perhaps you will be grateful.) [Al-Qasas: 71-73]

"Wa-ja'alna ash-shamsa 'alayhi daleelan" (And We made the sun as a guide for it)—just as the proof of anything opens and clarifies it, similarly the sun inside the global darkness of the night becomes a guide of the path, removing and opening it. If it does not show light, all would remain wandering; no one would know whether there is any path to go out from this doorless dome!

The benefits of night and day:
This has directed attention to the signs of day and night from another angle. He said: "He is the God who made the night as a covering for you, you wear its comfortable sheet to sleep for rest, and He made your sleep as a rest which makes you fresh and active anew to take part in the activities of life, then made the day as rising for you to seek God's provision and favor, and it also, every morning, reminds of the rising on the Day of Resurrection—that in the manner you rise in the morning after sleeping in the night, you will rise alive on the morning of Resurrection." The word "nushoor" (rising) here, with great beauty, points to the life after death. The meaning is: if there are seeing eyes and thinking intellects, in the daily coming and going of night and day and their benefits are so many signs of God's power, mercy, providence, wisdom, Monotheism, and Resurrection, which are sufficient to prove every word of the Quran, but these signs are beneficial for those inside whom is the desire to think and obtain lesson. Those inside whom this desire is not present remain blind to all these signs, demanding new signs. Forward, this theme is also coming in verse 64, and will come in verses 86-87 of Surah An-Naml too, from which its other aspects also will come to light.

Verses 48-49:
Pointer to the signs of rain:
By "rahmah" (mercy) here is meant rain, and this word has entered in this meaning in places in the Quran. After directing attention to those attributes of Allah of which the coming and going of night and day is the witness, He directed attention to the mercy of rain, which indeed does not come every day, but every living thing of this earth is in need of it; when it comes, it makes us observe all those attributes and majesties of the Lord on which the call of the Quran is founded.

He said: "Your Lord is He who, before the rain of mercy, sends the winds of season as glad tidings which bring caravans of clouds from various directions, then join them together, then where He commands, those clouds send down pure and purifying water, by which the dead earth receives fresh life, and many of God's creations—beasts and humans—are watered from it."

The meaning is: ponder: has this world, whose every corner displays such wisdoms and powers, come into existence without some Creator? Is all this merely the miracle of some blind and deaf cause? Has this connection between cloud, wind, rain, and human and animal needs from the sky to the earth been created of its own accord? Is it possible to assume, after observing this compatibility of opposites, that multiple wills are active in it? Does this care of mercy and providence not impose some responsibility upon man from the Merciful Lord? Does every resurrection of the earth after rain not remind of the reality: for the Wise and Powerful who is showing this observation of His power and wisdom, raising people after their death and turning to dust is not difficult at all?

By bringing the attribute "katheer" (abundant) with humans and beasts, the purpose is to point to the reality: although among the creations of Allah are those whom He has not made in need of water, many creations are those which are in need of water for their life. Among them, He specifically mentioned beasts and humans, who are among the prominent and superior terrestrial creations. With their mention, their accessories and other creations naturally entered under them.

Verses 50-52:
An address to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him):
Here, context is proof: the referent of the object pronoun is the Quran, in support of which the above arguments have been explained. This is, in the middle of the mention of signs, an address to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "As far as arguments are concerned, We have clearly explained them through this Quran in various styles among the people, but the majority of them, instead of appreciating this blessing, persist in ungratefulness and denial; so now, neither is there any shortcoming in your struggle, nor in Our care of reminder and teaching; rather, all the shortcoming is of these people themselves."

Various aspects of the Quran's arguments:
The explanation of the meaning of "tasreef" has passed in another place: the Quran has explained every reality in so many various styles that only an obstinate and rigid can deny them. From "baynahum" (among them), the point emerged: the completion of proof has been done upon every class and every group of the nation. It has not happened that this struggle of preaching and teaching remained limited inside some specific group.

"Wa law shi'na laba'athna fee kulli qaryatin nadheeran"—meaning: "If We saw that because of your being alone, there was some lack in the work of calling and preaching, We would have sent a warner in every town. This work was not difficult for Us. But the lack is not from your side; rather, all the corruption is inside these people themselves who have closed their ears and suspended their minds."

"Fala tuti'i al-kafireena wa-jahidhum bihi jihadan kabeeran"—meaning, do not care in the least about these new demands they are presenting before you merely to corner you. Rather, continue in this Jihad with the weapon of this Quran. In "jihadan kabeeran" (a great Jihad), there is a pointer: though this Jihad is highly keth-an, if some punishment were sent down upon them according to their demand, the decision of the entire dispute could occur in the blink of an eye, but the wisdom of Allah is: instead of the divine punishment, through this Book, if anyone has any spark of accepting the truth, let him accept it, and let no excuse remain for anyone before God.

"Ata'at" here is in the meaning of listening to someone's words and considering them. In another place, we have explained it.

The rein of the opposites of the universe is in the hand of God:
"Marj" means leaving, and "furat" means highly sweet and pleasant.

The explanation of "hijran mahjooran" has passed under verse 22. Here, this is in the meaning of a strong barrier and secure wall. "Hijran mahjooran" is a structure of the same kind as "dillan daleelan", etc.

After the verses of address, this has again directed attention to a great sign of Allah's power and wisdom among the signs of the horizons: He is the God who, on one hand, leaves the river of sweet water, and on the other hand, the sea of salty water; the waves of both collide, but despite this, it does not happen that the sweet water becomes salty or the salty water sweet; rather, both maintain their characteristic temperaments within their limits. The Powerful and Wise God places such an unseen wall between them which is neither visible, nor can their mutual collision break it. This is clear testimony: all the opposites in this universe are under the control of a higher Wise and Powerful; He gives respite to whomsoever He intends, and stops wherever He intends. It is not possible for them to deviate by a hair's breadth from His prescribed limits. It should be kept in mind: from the existence of opposites, the polytheistic nations faced the greatest misguidance. They considered this universe a battlefield of opposites and began the worship of every opposite, establishing it as a deity. The Quran has refuted this delusion in various styles, whose explanation we have done in their places. Here too, attention is directed to this reality in a new style: the opposites in this universe are not unbridled; rather, it is clearly seen: their rein is in the hand of a higher power. To this, pointer is made in Surah Ar-Rahman too:
(He released the two seas, meeting [side by side]; Between them is a barrier [so] neither of them transgresses.) [Ar-Rahman: 19-20]

A subjective proof of the compatibility in opposites:
"Nasaban wa-sihran"—meaning, possessing "nasab" (lineage) and "sihr" (marriage-relation). The meaning of lineage is clear. "Sihr" refers to that relationship which comes into existence through marriage. The meaning is: He created man from water, and from it created his partner too, then deposited inside both those natural inclinations: man can establish lineage relationships on one hand, and link with marriage-relations on the other hand, and in this manner, build the family, society, and community for which the Wise and Powerful Creator gave him existence. "Wa-kana rabbuka qadeeran"—meaning, this power and choice belongs only to God: to bring opposites into existence from one single water, and then, by His power and wisdom, thread these opposites into one string. This is clear proof: the will of a single Wise and Powerful is active over this entire universe. He alone, by His power, brings opposites into existence from one single matter, and then, by His infinite wisdom, creates connection and joining inside these opposites.

It should be kept in mind: in the previous verses, objective proofs were explained, and this proof is among the subjective proofs.

Friendship with Satan and war against God:
The proofs and testimonies of the horizons and the souls indeed are proving: the Creator, Owner, and Controller of the universe is Allah, who is running its system with complete power and wisdom; but these foolish people are worshipping such things which can neither bring any benefit to them, nor any harm.

"Wa-kana al-kafiru 'ala rabbihi daheeran"—"al-kafir" (the disbeliever) is in the meaning of genus here, and by it are meant those polytheists whose mention has been coming down from above, because Shirk in its reality is disbelief. "Daheer" means helper, and when conjoined with "'ala", the meaning of adversary and opponent is born in it. The meaning is: the actual reality is indeed what has been explained, but these disbelievers have stood up as helpers of Satan and adversaries to their Lord, and are opposing this call to worship the One God with all their power, which God's Messenger is giving them.

Comfort to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him):
This is to comfort the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Your responsibility is warning and glad tidings. Convey your call to them, and warn them of the consequences of its denial. To accept it or not to accept it is their own responsibility. Regarding it, you will not be questioned."

That is: "Tell them clearly: I am not demanding any reward from you for this call, that my being affected by your acceptance or rejection should occur. I have conveyed the message of your Lord to you, and the purpose is only: whoever wants to adopt the path of his Lord may adopt it. If you adopt it, its benefit will be to you; if you do not, its consequence you will suffer. From this, no harm will reach me." This same theme has entered in Surah Saba' thus: (Say, "Whatever payment I might have asked of you—it is yours. My payment is only from Allah...") [Saba': 47].

Meaning: "Have trust in your Living God who does not die. He is the Living God, and will remain alive forever." Having trust in Him is never followed by deprivation and failure. In this is a subtle sarcasm on those dead deities of the polytheists regarding whom it was said: they can bring neither benefit nor harm.

"Wa-sabbihi bihamdihi" (And glorify Him with praise)—this is the medium to acquire patience and trust. In the Holy Quran, where the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is directed to patience and trust, there, maximum engagement in the glorification of God and the care for prayer are emphasized. We have discussed this in detail in its place.

"Wa-kafa bihi bidhunoobi 'ibadihi khabeeran"—meaning: "Refer the matter of these opponents of yours to your Lord."

He is well-aware of all their crimes, and when He is, He will deal with them as they deserve.

Pointer to some attributes of Allah:
These are the further attributes of that Living God who does not die: He created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days. By six days, as explained under Al-A'raf 54, is meant divine days whose length is known to Him alone. We can express them in our language as six epochs. In this is a pointer to the reality: this universe has not come as an accidental occurrence; rather, its Creator has created it with great care.

"Thumma istawa 'ala al-'arshi" (then He established Himself upon the Throne)—meaning, after creating this universe with this care, its Creator has not sat detached, as the fools assumed. If He had done so, this would have been a useless work, and it is completely contrary to the status of an administrator and wise to do a useless work. Rather, He is actively established on the Throne of sovereignty of this universe, supervising all its administration. The evolution which occurred in it has occurred not of its own accord, but by the design and wisdom of its Creator, and no other has any part in its administration.

"Ar-rahmanu fas'al bihi khabeeran"—"Ar-Rahman" (the Merciful) is the predicate, whose subject is assumed as omitted—meaning, He who gave existence to this universe is the Merciful. To concentrate the entire attention of the addressee on the predicate, the subject was omitted. By the mention of the attribute of the Merciful, the purpose is specifically to point to this universe being with purpose and goal: its Creator is the Merciful, and for His mercy alone He created it. The inevitable requirement of this attribute of His is: He must bring a day in which His mercy manifests with His perfect justice. The explanation of this theme has passed in its place.

God alone knows His attributes:
In "fas'al bihi khabeeran" (so ask of Him, as He is Acquainted), the address is to the common addressees with whom the discussion has been coming down, and the preposition of "bi" relates to "khabeer" (Acquainted). The preposition of "bi" comes with "sa'ala" (asked) only when this word contains the meaning of "istihza'" (mockery). This is not the place for it. By "khabeer" (Acquainted), Allah has pointed to His own Entity, and its indefinite form is to highlight its majesty. He said: "The true attributes of God are those which He has explained, not those which you have fabricated of your own accord to attribute to Him. If His attributes are to be known, do not guess, but ask of the Acquainted who is Himself most aware of His attributes." Exactly in this style, in Surah Fatir, it is said: (And on the Day of Resurrection they will deny your association. And none can inform you like He who is Acquainted.) [Fatir: 14]. It is obvious: by "khabeer" (Acquainted) here is meant Allah, who is informing people of the actual realities through His Book and His Messenger.

The objection of the Quraysh to the name "Ar-Rahman", and the answer to it:
That is, the greatest attribute of the Creator of this universe is the Merciful, but the state of these fools is: when they are called to prostrate and worship the Merciful God, they answer with great arrogance: "What is this Merciful? Shall we prostrate to whatever you command us?" "Wa zadahum nufooran" (And it increases them in aversion)—meaning, instead of being pleased with the guidance to the true Object and appreciating it, this thing increases their aversion and detachment.

Under verse 110 of Surah Al-Isra, we have explained in detail: the Arabs, although they were not unfamiliar with the name "Ar-Rahman" for God—both "Allah" and "Ar-Rahman" are found in their literature—this name "Ar-Rahman" was more well-known among the People of the Book, and the pagan Arabs mostly spoke the name "Allah". The leaders of the Quraysh made this thing also an excuse to instigate their nation against the Quran. It has passed behind: they tried to create this bad-thinking against the Quran: in its composition, some scholars of the People of the Book are also partners. In proof of this, they presented the word "Ar-Rahman", saying: "Look, in this Book, the word Ar-Rahman enters repeatedly. This is proof: some People of the Book, behind the veil of Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), want to impose their religious concepts on us." In the atmosphere of bad-thinking, such rumors are highly effective in instigating the common folk. From this, excitement is born in both national and religious feelings; consequently, a severe kind of bias and hatred was born in many of the ignorant against the name "Ar-Rahman". The Quran has directed attention to this foolishness of theirs in Surah Al-Isra too, and here too, showing: "This name is a treasury of countless blessings and mercies for you. Why do you deprive yourselves of the blessings of this blessed name in opposing others!"

Verses 61-62:
The actual need is not of signs, but of the desire to benefit from signs:
After the verses of address, this has again directed attention to the signs of the horizons: as far as signs are concerned, there is no lack of them. The need is not of some new sign, but of the feeling and desire to benefit from signs. Those inside whom this feeling and desire is not present remain blind after seeing every kind of sign.

"Limani arada an yadhdhakkara aw arada shakooran"—remembrance (tadhakkur) is the act of the intellect, and gratitude (shukur) is of the heart. Allah has filled every corner of this universe with signs that awaken the intellect and the heart, but benefiting correctly from them depends on man's own desire, and for the trial of this desire, Allah created man, and on this depends all his honor—in this matter, He has not approved coercion.

Meaning of "Burooj":
"Ja'ala fee as-sama'i buroojan"—the explanation of "burooj" (constellations/towers) has passed under verse 16 of Surah Al-Hijr. In our view, by this are meant those towers and lookouts which Allah has made in this system of the universe so that the devils of jinn and humans may not advance beyond a specific limit. In these towers and lookouts is the guard of His close angels, who supervise the devils who try to steal hearing. This arrangement Allah has made so that inside the limits of the High Assembly, no one may have reach except His close angels. Only they enter those limits, and only they bring the news of His spirit and His inspired unseen to His prophets and messengers. Except this medium, there is no other medium to know the unseen; for this reason, other claimants of knowing the unseen—whether they are fortune-tellers or astrologers—are all liars... It should be kept in mind: the mention of this arrangement too was made by Allah with the same introduction of "Tabarak" with which the Surah began. This is a pointer to the reality: Allah being entirely good and blessed inevitably requires Him to protect the pure spring of revelation from every kind of satanic intrusion, because on this alone depends the life of the spirit and the heart, and all success and prosperity of this world and the Hereafter. Content yourself with these pointers here. A detailed discussion on this issue, if God wills, will come in the surahs of the last group.

"Khilfatan" has the same meaning as is expressed by "ikhtilaf al-layli wan-nahari" (alternation of night and day)—meaning, the rotation of night and day one after another; the realities of whose remembrance this rotation makes have been explained in other places.

The upcoming theme... Verses 63-77:
Conclusion of the Surah:
Forward are the concluding verses of the Surah, in which Allah has counted the attributes of those servants of His whose capabilities of the intellect and the heart are alive and who benefit from His signs and His sent-down Book. This is the picture of those who advanced to become believers in the Quran, and through this picture, the apparent and hidden of those who were spending all their strength in opposing the Quran and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) have been fully exposed... In this light, recite the upcoming verses.
