Meaning of "Hawn" and "Salam":
"Hawn" means humility and modesty, and "salam" (peace), in the manner it is for welcoming, is also for those occasions when to separate from someone with beauty and civility and to escape from him is intended. The greeting Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) offered on separating from Azar was of this kind.

The description of "'Ibad ar-Rahman" after "'Ibad ash-Shayateen":
In the previous verses has passed the mention of those rebels who, in the intoxication of pride and arrogance, were ready neither to hear any word nor to understand, to the extent that even the beloved name of God "Ar-Rahman" became a cause of anger for them, they were not tolerant of hearing it. Now, in contrast to them, these attributes of the "'Ibad ar-Rahman" (servants of the Merciful) are described, whose purpose, on one hand, is to place such a mirror before the arrogant of Quraysh which fully exposes the actual beauty of humanity before them, so that their own apparent and hidden defects may be clearly clarified to them; and on the other hand, to clarify to the Muslims in a highly excellent style: what path the servants of the Merciful must adopt in this world, and with what virtues Allah wants to see their apparent and hidden adorned.

Arrogance is the Sunnah of Satan:
According to the demand of the context, first of all, the attribute referenced here is humility and modesty: He said: Our servants do not walk with pride and arrogance in the land; rather, they walk with extreme humility—this walking in humility is mentioned as a reflection of their hidden. The actual purpose is the expression of the reality: such an awe of God's majesty and grandeur remains on their hearts that this thing is displayed from their every step which they place on the earth. If arrogance is inside a person, indeed it is displayed from his every movement, but his walk specifically is its manifestation; for this reason, He specifically referenced it here. Meant, in reality, is what we have pointed: their hearts are pure from arrogance. The reality of arrogance is: to behave proudly against the truth. If some person bows his head before the truth, whether it is small or great, he is included in the "'Ibad ar-Rahman", even if he walks with his chest out and his head high in the support and honor of the truth; and if he behaves proudly against the truth, he is of the allies of Satan, even if he creates artificial humility in his walk. It should be kept in mind: arrogance is the Sunnah of Iblis, and this is that primary blemish having which the growth of any virtue inside a person is impossible.

To dispute with the uncivilized is contrary to the status of "'Ibad ar-Rahman":
"Wa-idha khatabahumu al-jahiloona qaloo salaman"—"mukhatabah" here is in the meaning of disputing. That is: when such uncivilized and bad-mannered people, whose criticisms of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) have been mentioned above, try to dispute with them and make them the target of their mockery, they act on the Abrahamic Sunnah, and instead of disputing with them, offer greeting and depart. Greeting, as we have pointed out above, is a civilized and blessed method of separating from someone. If separation is chosen in this manner, it is expected: if some spark of good is inside him, he will review his attitude and turn to hearing and understanding instead of disputing; and if there is no spark of good inside him and he is merely desirous of disputing, then addressing such a person is not worthy of the "'Ibad ar-Rahman". This theme will come in detail in verse 55 of Surah Al-Qasas.

Verses 64-66:
The private life of "'Ibad ar-Rahman":
In the above verse, pointer was made to a specific aspect of their public life. In this verse, light is shed on their private life. He said: "They spend their nights in prostration and standing before their Lord." The desire and anxiety displayed from the style of expression "sujjadan wa-qiyaman" is beyond description—it is obvious: by this are meant not merely the obligatory prayers, but this is the expression of the night-awakening and anxiety of the Tahajjud prayers' prostrations and standings. The meaning is: these people spend their nights neither in places of luxury nor sleep in soft and comfortable beds carefree of this world and the Hereafter; rather, rising in the nights, they pray to be saved from the punishment of Hell.

"Inna 'adhabaha kana gharaman"—"gharam" refers to something which is inseparable, attached. This segment is by way of confirmation and support from Allah of that fear because of which they spend their nights in this anxiety and unrest. The meaning is: if these people rise in the nights to seek refuge from the punishment of Hell in this manner, they are doing completely correctly and with highly correct future-thinking, because the punishment of Hell is such an inseparable thing that escaping from it will be impossible. To be saved from it, whatever can be done can be done only in this life, and only by the help of God.

"Innaha sa'at mustaqarran wa-muqaman"—"mustaqarr" and "muqam", though used as exchangeable words, when used together, some difference is born between them. From pondering over their usages, the result I have reached is: in "mustaqarr", the meaning of permanent staying-place is found, and "muqam" comes for temporary staying-place too. The meaning is: Hell is such a terrifying place that let alone as a permanent staying-place, even as a temporary staying-place, it is not worthy of being tolerated, whereas some bad place also can be such that, from some aspect, it can be tolerated temporarily.

Care for spending:
This is a pointer to the care found inside them for spending in the path of Allah. He said: "When they spend on their needs, they maintain caution and economy in it. Neither do they let extravagance find way, nor miserliness. They know that in their wealth, there are rights of others too." For this reason, they do not let their needs exceed their limits, so that they can perform the rights of others. Although for a middle-class life, it is difficult to prescribe one specific standard—difference can occur in it according to the change of situations—this difference is not so subtle that a person cannot know it. Keeping in view the standard of his nation and his society, every owner of wealth can estimate: what standard of life he should maintain so that he can serve his other poor brothers and his religion too. In this matter, the pattern our righteous predecessors left for us is: instead of giving allowance to one's soul, the dominant trend of a person must remain toward the other aspect—meaning, instead of falling into the delusion of raising one's standard of life, he should spend in the path of Allah as much as possible. Spending as much as possible in this path is no extravagance. As for those who fall into the delusion of raising their standard of life—they become brothers of the devils, and they are never given the success of performing the rights of God and His servants. In whichever society this disease becomes common, ultimately the punishment of communism is imposed on it.

Abstaining from crimes:
By "atham", Al-Jawhari has meant the result of the sin. This is a usage of the kind when some action or noun is brought, and the purpose from it is its fruit and result. In "sayalqawna ghayyan" [Maryam: 59] too, this same style is maintained. He said: "Neither do they associate any partner with their Lord, nor do they kill any soul unjustly, nor do they commit fornication." In "an-nafsa allatee harrama Allahu illa bil-haqqi", this reality is clarified: Allah has made every soul sacred and honored, and transgression upon it is unlawful, unless it commits some crime because of which it is deprived of this protection of the divine law. All these three crimes—Shirk, murder, and fornication—are among the greatest crimes of the chain of God's rights and the servants' rights. On this, all heavenly religions are agreed. He said: the servants of the Merciful God do not go near these crimes. Those who are committing them, let them remember: they will inevitably face their end.

"Yuda'af lahu al-'adhabu yawma al-qiyamah wa-yakhlud feehi muhanan" (Multiplied for him will be the punishment on the Day of Resurrection, and he will abide therein humiliated) [69].

Meaning of "Mudâ'afah":
"Mudâ'afah" means adding to something, regardless of whether this addition is double or more; and from usages, it is also found: inside it, the meaning of gradual addition is also present, just as it is in "la ta'kuloo ar-riba ad'afan mudâ'afah" [Al-Imran: 130].

This is the description of the result of the crimes mentioned in the above verse. He said: "Those who commit any of these crimes will suffer their punishment in the Resurrection in such a manner that gradually, addition will be made to their punishment. It will not happen that after suffering some punishment, they will be released, or slowly some ease will occur in their punishment; rather, they will abide in this punishment forever in a state of extreme humiliation." To the punishment, the addition of humiliation has doubled and multiplied its severity. A punishment can be such that it is a punishment but there is no humiliation in it; but in this punishment, there will be the blow of humiliation too. It should be kept in mind: these matters are being said to the arrogant of Quraysh, and the actual punishment of arrogance is eternal humiliation.

Verses 70-71:
The effects of repentance:
He said: "From this punishment of the results of actions, only those will remain safe who, making repentance and reform, adopt the path of faith and righteous action." On the conditions and manners of repentance, we have discussed in detail under verses 17-18 of Surah An-Nisa. The meaning of "yubaddilu Allahu sayyi'atihim hasanatin" (Allah will replace their evil deeds with good deeds) is: those who make repentance to adopt the life of faith and righteous action—their virtues erase the past sins of their record of deeds. Allah places their virtues in their place, which cover their sins.

Great glad tidings for those who make repentance:
"Wa man taba wa-'amila salihan fa-innahu yatoobu ila Allahi mataban"—this segment is completely in contrast to "wa-man yaf'al dhalika yalqa athaman" above, and in it are great glad tidings for those who make repentance. The meaning is: those who die with their sins will indeed face their sins, but those who make repentance will return to their Lord with great honor. The emphasis in "mataban" is to highlight its majesty—meaning, this return will be of extreme honor and majesty. In the parable of the lost sheep, with great eloquence, our master Jesus has explained how much Allah is pleased with the repentance of His repenting servant, and with what degree of His grace and favor He exalts him. This same is clarified by this verse.

In this is also an aspect of encouraging for repentance: often, a person hesitates to leave the life of sin because he assumes: if he leaves this life, he will have to search for a new environment for himself, which is a highly difficult work. This verse comforts such people: those who leave evil and the evil environment obtain the company and patronage of Allah, and those who obtain this are independent of everything.

Abstaining from vanities:
"Zoor" means lie and falsehood, and "laghw" refers to those statements and works which are not worthy of chaste and serious people. He said: "Our these servants do not join in any false work, and if they have to pass by some vain thing, they pass by it with extreme dignity and nobility, in the manner a clean person passes by a dirty place." In verse 55 of Surah Al-Qasas, this same is stated thus: (And when they hear ill speech, they turn away from it and say, "For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds. Peace be upon you; we seek not the ignorant.")

Sarcasm on the opponents of the Quran:
In this verse, there is praise for these servants of Allah, and also, alongside, a sarcasm on those blind and deaf opponents of the Quran who listened to the Quran merely for objection and criticism. He said: "Our these servants, when they are taught and reminded through the Quran, benefit from it; they do not fall upon it blind and deaf to oppose it." This theme will come in detail in Surah Al-Jinn, verse 19, and Surah Al-Ma'arij, verses 36-37.

Worry for the end of the household:
These people, along with worry for their own end, also carry worry for the end of their household and their offspring. They are acquainted with the reality: "Each of you is a shepherd, and each of you is responsible for his flock." Because of having the due sense of this responsibility, they do not lead their life inside their household like those careless people who only care for the luxury of their world, having no concern with whether their household is walking on the path of virtue or evil. The mention of these careless people will come in Surah Al-Qiyamah and Surah Al-Mutaffifeen. The state of the servants of God is completely different: they are always anxious lest their household adopt the path of Satan. In verse 26 of Surah At-Tur, "Inna kunna qablu fee ahlina mushfiqeena" (Indeed, we were previously among our people fearful), pointer is made to this very worry and anxiety of theirs. Because of this anxiety, they are always praying to their Lord: "O Lord! Grant us coolness of eyes from our wives and our offspring—let their deeds and morals be according to Your approval and our yearns; and let us be leaders of the righteous in this world, and rise as leaders of the righteous in the Hereafter too, not as leaders of the wicked and disobedient."

From the translation we have made of this verse, it is clear: this is no prayer for obtaining leadership and power, but is the prayer for performing the responsibilities of that leadership which every head of a family actually possesses. We have written the commentary of this verse in our book 'Towdeehat' too. Those seeking detail may read it.

Verses 75-76:
The reward of humility is the high status of Paradise:
He said: "Those who are of the aforementioned attributes will obtain the high chambers of Paradise in reward for these attributes and actions of theirs." They led a life of humility and modesty in the world; for this reason, they will be worthy of the high status of Paradise. Our master Jesus said: "Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven."

From "bima sabaroo" (because they patiently endured), this fact became clear: developing these attributes and maintaining them is no easy game; rather, for it, great patience and steadfastness is necessary. "Wa yulaqqawna feeha tahiyyatan wa-salaman"—meaning, those who succeed in this trial of patience will indeed be worthy: the angels of God welcome them with congratulations and peace.

"Hasanat mustaqarran wa-muqaman"—opposite to the words used above for Hell, these words have entered for Paradise. The meaning is: this is beautiful as a staying-place and beautiful as a temporary place too!

At the end, this is a threat to the opponents. Addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), He said: "Inform these people: I am after you in this manner not because any work of God is stopped or will be stopped without you. What care can my Lord have for you or anyone! The purpose is only to invite you to the truth, and in this, the benefit is entirely yours, not mine or my Lord's. Now, when you have denied this invitation, be ready for that end which is inevitable. Very soon, you will deal with it."

Remember here that Divine Sunnah which Allah has prescribed for those who deny the messengers.

By the success granted by God, on these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed.
